label: Vicarious Liability
scenario_patterns: A delivery driver causes an accident while making deliveries for his employer
A security guard assaults a visitor while on duty at a commercial building
A clinic nurse administers the wrong medication to a patient during working hours
An independent contractor hired by a company causes property damage at a worksite
legal_tests: There must be a relationship of employer and employee (as distinct from principal and independent contractor)
The tortious act must have been committed in the course of employment
The Salmond test: the act was either authorised by the employer, or was an unauthorised mode of performing an authorised act
Close connection test: whether the wrongful act was so closely connected with the employment that it would be fair and just to hold the employer liable
The employer-employee relationship is determined by the multi-factor test including control, integration, and economic reality
common_fact_patterns: Road accidents by employees driving company vehicles or making deliveries
Assaults or torts by security guards, bouncers, or enforcement officers during duty
Medical negligence by hospital employees — nurses, junior doctors, or technicians
Fraud or misrepresentation by employees in the course of business dealings
Torts by gig economy workers — whether they are employees or independent contractors
sg_case_references: Ng Huat Seng v Munib Mohammad Madni [2017] SGCA 58 — vicarious liability of employer for employee's negligence
Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken AB (Publ) v Asia Pacific Breweries (Singapore) Pte Ltd [2011] SGCA 22 — vicarious liability for employee's fraud and the close connection test
Keppel Bus Co Ltd v Sa'ad bin Ahmad [1974] 1 MLJ 191 — employer's vicarious liability for bus driver's negligence
Jet Holding Ltd v Cooper Cameron (Singapore) Pte Ltd [2006] SGCA 20 — distinction between employee and independent contractor
party_roles: plaintiff/claimant harmed by the employee's tort
employee/tortfeasor who committed the wrongful act
defences: The tortfeasor was an independent contractor, not an employee
The tortious act was not committed in the course of employment — it was a frolic of the employee's own
The employee's act was wholly outside the scope of authorised duties with no close connection to employment
Contributory negligence of the plaintiff reducing the employer's liability
The employee acted in direct contravention of an express prohibition that limited the scope of employment
